name: Mohoidae
description: Mohoidae is an extinct family of passerine birds endemic to the Hawaiian Islands. These honeyeaters were driven to extinction by habitat loss, introduced predators, and disease in the 19th and 20th centuries.

children.name: Moho
children.description: An extinct genus of Hawaiian honeyeaters known for their mostly black plumage and yellow tufts. All species were driven to extinction by the early 20th century.

children.name: Moho apicalis
children.description: The Oahu Oo was a black honeyeater with white tail tips and yellow thigh tufts endemic to Oahu. It was last recorded in 1837 and is now extinct due to habitat destruction and introduced diseases.

children.name: Moho braccatus
children.description: The Kauai Oo was a small black honeyeater with yellow leg feathers and a distinctive call. It was the last surviving Moho species, with the final confirmed sighting in 1987.

children.name: Moho nobilis
children.description: The Hawaii Oo was the largest of the Moho honeyeaters, with glossy black plumage and prominent yellow tufts. It was last recorded in the 1890s on the Big Island of Hawaii.

children.name: Chaetoptila
children.description: An extinct monotypic genus of Hawaiian honeyeater with a distinctive long, curved bill.

children.name: Chaetoptila angustipluma
children.description: The Kioea was a brownish honeyeater with a long, decurved bill adapted for nectar feeding. It was endemic to the Big Island of Hawaii and went extinct in the mid-19th century, with only a few museum specimens remaining.
